scenario: Scenario based on: How to Set Up Burp Proxy
chapter: How to Set Up Burp Proxy

steps.observation: Initial state: preparing to begin
steps.thought: Based on the current state, the next action should address: Begin by downloading and installing the app. Since it is a java app, you may nee...
steps.action: Begin by downloading and installing the app. Since it is a java app, you may need to install java JRE. To ensure that your browser uses Burp, you have to configure a few settings. The recommendation is to use Firefox with Burp because by doing so, you will be able to set it up without having to make any changes to the system wide settings which would affect a couple of programs. Once you have downloaded, installed, and started Burp, click 'proxy tab' and then 'options.' Ensure the 'proxy listeners' is running and note the interface, which by default, is 127.0.0.1:8080. After that, move down to the sections of and and ensure that the top level and have been checked. In addition, check the third checkbox under that says 'or request was intercepted.' The settings should be similar to the ones below. This will enable Burp to capture both the browser requests and the responses of the server. Next, we have to setup Firefox so that it can use Burp as a proxy. Just click on 'Firefox' and after that 'preferences.' Click the advanced icon and then the network button. As shown below: The last step will be to change network settings. How do you do that? Well, under Connection, proceed to configure how Mozilla Firefox will be connecting to the internet by first clicking on the settings button.
steps.expected_result: Ready for next step: Adjust the settings to match the picture below....

steps.expected_result: Ready for next step: Check the box to 'use this proxy server for all protocols.' Finally, do away with the settings in th...

steps.action: Check the box to 'use this proxy server for all protocols.' Finally, do away with the settings in the box that states 'no proxy for' so that you can capture the local traffic.
steps.expected_result: Ready for next step: Click ok and you are set to begin. At this point, you have to test your setup to make sure it works....

steps.observation: Current state after step 4
steps.thought: Based on the current state, the next action should address: Click ok and you are set to begin. At this point, you have to test your setup to...
steps.action: Click ok and you are set to begin. At this point, you have to test your setup to make sure it works. Go back to Firefox and key in google.com and then click enter. If everything is set up correctly, your browser should hang there waiting for the website. After this, when Burp has captured your request, return to Burp expecting to see the HTTP request in the proxy to Google, then intercept the tab.
steps.expected_result: Ready for next step: Ensure you are looking at the right screen on Burp. There are very many options but it should genera...

steps.observation: Current state after step 5
steps.thought: Based on the current state, the next action should address: Ensure you are looking at the right screen on Burp. There are very many options ...
steps.action: Ensure you are looking at the right screen on Burp. There are very many options but it should generally look like this: Send the request to the server by clicking the forward button. You should receive the server response almost immediately.
steps.expected_result: Ready for next step: Click the forward button once more and send to the browser the server response. The server response ...

steps.observation: Current state after step 6
steps.thought: Based on the current state, the next action should address: Click the forward button once more and send to the browser the server response. ...
steps.action: Click the forward button once more and send to the browser the server response. The server response to our original request to Google is a redirection of 301, which will inform your browser the location header to go to www.google.com. The browser makes this automatic request for you so you can safely forward the request and the response. Google will once again redirect to the SSL version of Google, which will definitely present another issue. For Burp to connect to the SSL sites, it will make an interception to the connection and gives its own SSL certificate to the browser. This enables Burp to decrypt the HTTP request and response even if SSL is in use. The browser is however smart enough to tell whether the SSL certificate is okay or not to provide a warning to the user if the SSL certificate is valid and will give a warning to the user about the certificate being invalid for this site. Now that we know Burp is intercepting the request, you can click on the 'I understand the risks' and the 'add exception' to add the Burps SSL certificate. You can then click 'confirm security exception' so that the browser will let you use Burp for this SSL connection. When accepting this, take care and ensure you are using Burp, otherwise, you do not add the exception. The browser now makes the SSL request, and Burp captures it once again. Just keep forwarding the responses and requests until you see the Google homepage on the browser. If you've done everything we've learnt so far, your appetite for moving a little further should be at its highest. Let's move on to the next chapter to take this a little further.
steps.expected_result: Procedure completed

scenario: Scenario based on: Whetting Your Hacking Appetite: Common Hacking Attacks
chapter: Whetting Your Hacking Appetite: Common Hacking Attacks

steps.observation: Initial state: preparing to begin
steps.thought: Based on the current state, the next action should address: Which common attacks do hackers use to hack into a system? You need to understan...
steps.action: Which common attacks do hackers use to hack into a system? You need to understand these attacks so you can test your sites and then code for these weaknesses. Many hackers direct a brute force attacks on website login page where they try thousands of passwords and usernames until they key in a correct combination. Brute force attacks compromise the very concept applied to resetting passwords, the secret questions, promotional and discount codes, and other information that is secret and used to reveal the identity of the user. To perform brute attacks, you will need the following: Confirm the account lockout-the request throttling is disabled or simple to bypass. Decide the username's format Make a list of the potential usernames Confirm the valid usernames Run tests on the passwords for every valid username Begin by deciding whether an account lockout exists. You can do this by failing the login for a user. Next, determine the format of the username. These can be from one site or another; nevertheless, the current trend is to use an email address, which is easier to remember and it can come in handy when conducting password resets. Assume the site you are targeting has such a login page as the one below.
steps.expected_result: Ready for next step: Notice that the username is an email address, otherwise, if the login screen did not tell us that, y...

steps.observation: Current state after step 1
steps.thought: Based on the current state, the next action should address: Notice that the username is an email address, otherwise, if the login screen did...
steps.action: Notice that the username is an email address, otherwise, if the login screen did not tell us that, you would have to determine that by registering or signing up for an account. Obviously, from the signup page, you can tell that the username is an email address. If you are dealing with a large public site, people usually sign up with yahoo, G-mail, and other popular email domains. It is rather unfortunate that because internet hacking is popular, presently, it is easy to get long lists of email addresses from compromised databases. Take this example; if you want to target Franco James, you will first key in jamesfranco@gmail.com (or his email account) followed by a password before you click login. You will probably get an error message stating that the email (jamesfranco@gmail.com) does not exist.
steps.expected_result: Procedure completed

steps.action: Try each one of them out. You will find that at least one of them will work. When you make a correct username guess, you will get an error message about the password being incorrect. However, having a valid email address is a good step to breaking in. Ergo Usernames are email addresses and the application will inform you whether or not the address is valid. You may find a valid email address but that contains a wrong password, and therefore, an 'incorrect password' message will appear. Since the application is a corporate HR, you will be right to guess that most users have the @onemonthsimple.com as the email. You will use this to create your own list of common names to find new users. It may take a while to guess the usernames; therefore, an attacker would make the process automatic, which is, trying usernames and matching the error messages with the valid ones.

scenario: Scenario based on: Conclusion

steps.observation: Initial state: preparing to begin
steps.thought: Based on the current state, the next action should address: I hope the book has taught you something about hacking. Learning how to hack is ...
steps.action: I hope the book has taught you something about hacking. Learning how to hack is a handy skill whether you are a security professional or not because it helps you implement the toughest security practices possible. Learning how to hack is as much about finding security weaknesses and fixing them as it is about anticipating them. To resolve hacking issues preemptively, it is important to learn the hacking methods black hat hackers use to penetrate systems. If you lack such knowledge, you will definitely have a hard time securing computer systems. Think of the computer network as a yard that has a fence around it to prevent people from getting in. You have something valuable in the yard that someone may want to steal. Ethical hacking comes in as a measure to check for weaknesses inside the yard and around the fence so you can reinforce the weak areas before anyone attempts to gain access. Today, very many business operations depend on the understanding of software-related risks made vulnerable to hacking. Even beyond business, the average person should have a clear understanding of the role of a hacker. Cloud computing, mobile technology, and the internet have changed our daily reality. As individuals, you are part of a bigger global online network; this exposes you to cybercrime and threats. In the face of cyber-attacks, there is great need to have more resilient computer systems. It is, therefore, prudent to gain a deep knowledge of the hacker's tactics and methodsas a precondition. Thank you again for downloading this book! I hope this book was able to help you to understand how to be a hacker (especially an ethical hacker). The next step is to implement what you have learnt. Finally, if you enjoyed this book, would you be kind enough to leave a review for this book on Amazon?

scenario: A client organisation asks you to perform a network penetration test. You must establish the legal and technical framework before any testing begins.

steps.observation: Client requests a security assessment of their corporate network (192.168.0.0/16).
steps.thought: No testing can begin without explicit written authorisation. I must define the scope, timeline, and rules of engagement first.
steps.action: Draft a Statement of Work (SoW) specifying: in-scope IP ranges, out-of-scope systems (production databases), testing window (off-peak hours), and emergency stop procedure.
steps.expected_result: Signed SoW from authorised client representative, with clear scope boundaries.

steps.observation: SoW is signed. Client provides a list of in-scope IP addresses and contact details for the emergency stop.
steps.thought: I need to confirm I have the right point-of-contact and that all team members are covered by the authorisation letter.
steps.action: Obtain a signed authorisation letter naming all testers by name/company. Confirm out-of-band emergency contact (direct phone, not email) with the client's CISO.
steps.expected_result: Named authorisation letter. Emergency contact established.

steps.observation: All documentation is in place. Testing window starts Monday 02:00–06:00 UTC.
steps.thought: I should confirm the technical constraints: are there any WAFs, IDS/IPS systems, or fragile legacy systems the client has flagged?
steps.action: Run a pre-engagement questionnaire to identify: known fragile systems, third-party cloud providers (AWS, Azure) with their own ToS, and whether social engineering is in scope.
steps.expected_result: List of constraints and exclusions appended to the SoW.

scenario: With authorisation in hand, gather intelligence about the target organisation's external footprint using only passive and semi-passive techniques.

steps.observation: Target: Acme Corp, domain acmecorp.example. Scope: external-facing assets only.
steps.thought: Start with fully passive OSINT — no packets to the target yet. I want DNS records, certificate transparency logs, and publicly exposed infrastructure.
steps.action: Query DNS (MX, SPF, DMARC, DKIM records), search certificate transparency logs (crt.sh) for subdomains, and review WHOIS registration history.
steps.expected_result: List of subdomains: mail.acmecorp.example, vpn.acmecorp.example, dev.acmecorp.example. SPF record reveals third-party email provider (SendGrid).

steps.observation: dev.acmecorp.example resolves to a public IP. This was not listed in scope explicitly.
steps.thought: Before scanning this host, I must verify with the client whether dev is in scope. Scanning an out-of-scope host — even accidentally — is a contract violation.
steps.action: Pause. Email and call the client contact to clarify whether dev.acmecorp.example is in scope. Document the exchange.
steps.expected_result: Client confirms dev is in scope and owned by them. SoW addendum signed.

steps.observation: Scope confirmed. Now perform semi-passive reconnaissance.
steps.thought: Semi-passive means sending packets that look like normal traffic. I can use Shodan, LinkedIn (for employee enumeration), and job postings to infer technology stack.
steps.action: Search Shodan for the target IP ranges. Review LinkedIn for job postings mentioning specific technologies (e.g., 'SAP ERP', 'Cisco ASA'). Check Wayback Machine for old login portals.
steps.expected_result: Shodan reveals port 8443 open on vpn.acmecorp.example running an older Palo Alto GlobalProtect version. LinkedIn reveals the ops team uses Ansible and Terraform.

scenario: Use gathered intelligence to model the most realistic threat actors and map likely attack paths before any active scanning.

steps.observation: Client is a financial services firm. Intelligence suggests external VPN portal, web application, and email are primary attack surfaces.
steps.thought: Who would realistically attack this client? Nation-state APTs targeting financial data, ransomware groups, and opportunistic credential stuffers.
steps.action: Build a threat actor matrix: (1) APT financial theft — high sophistication, low noise; (2) Ransomware-as-a-Service — commodity tools, phishing entry; (3) Insider threat — legitimate access abuse. Map each to likely entry points.
steps.expected_result: Prioritised attack path list: VPN credential stuffing (highest risk), phishing → Office macro → internal pivot, SQL injection on web app.

steps.observation: VPN portal is the highest-priority target based on threat model.
steps.thought: The GlobalProtect version found on Shodan has known CVEs. But before exploiting, I need to verify the version is accurate and understand the blast radius.
steps.action: Map the blast radius: if VPN is compromised, what is the attacker's position in the network? Is it DMZ-only or does it provide direct access to the internal LAN? Ask the client for a basic network diagram.
steps.expected_result: VPN grants access to internal LAN (192.168.10.0/24). High blast radius confirmed — this is the primary test target.

scenario: Actively scan in-scope systems to identify exploitable vulnerabilities, documenting all findings with severity ratings.

steps.observation: Active scanning phase begins within the authorised testing window.
steps.thought: Start with a lightweight port scan to avoid overwhelming fragile systems, then escalate to service fingerprinting.
steps.action: Run Nmap SYN scan on in-scope IP ranges with rate limiting (--max-rate 500). Record open ports, services, and banner versions.
steps.expected_result: Port 443 (HTTPS), 8443 (GlobalProtect), 22 (SSH), 3389 (RDP) found open across multiple hosts.

steps.observation: RDP (3389) is exposed on an internal host visible through VPN.
steps.thought: RDP exposure is high risk — brute force and BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) are common attack vectors. Check if the system is patched.
steps.action: Use Nmap scripts (rdp-enum-encryption, rdp-vuln-ms12-020) to fingerprint RDP configuration. Check if NLA is enforced. Cross-reference OS version against known CVEs.
steps.expected_result: RDP running on Windows Server 2016, NLA not enforced, MS12-020 not applicable but NLA missing is a credential exposure risk.

steps.observation: Web application on port 443 identified. Login portal found.
steps.thought: Web application vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10) are the most common findings. Start with automated scan, then manual verification.
steps.action: Run Nikto and OWASP ZAP against the web app. Manually test for SQL injection in login form, check for missing security headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS).
steps.expected_result: Missing HSTS header. Login form vulnerable to SQL injection (error-based). No account lockout after failed attempts.

scenario: With client authorisation and documented vulnerabilities, attempt controlled exploitation to prove impact and demonstrate the attack chain.

steps.observation: SQL injection confirmed in login form. Client has authorised exploitation to prove impact.
steps.thought: Use the minimum payload necessary to demonstrate the vulnerability without causing data corruption or service disruption.
steps.action: Use sqlmap in read-only mode (--technique=E --no-update) to extract the list of database tables. Do NOT dump actual user data — only confirm that extraction is possible.
steps.expected_result: Confirmed: attacker can enumerate all 14 database tables including 'users', 'transactions', 'audit_log'. Impact: full database read access.

steps.observation: Exploitation successful. Now demonstrate post-exploitation impact within scope.
steps.thought: Post-exploitation shows the client what an attacker would do AFTER gaining initial access. This justifies remediation priority.
steps.action: Establish a Meterpreter session (with client authorisation). Run 'hashdump' to show credential harvesting is possible. Check for network shares and unencrypted credentials in memory (mimikatz simulation).
steps.expected_result: NTLM hashes retrieved for 3 service accounts. Two mapped network drives found with world-readable sensitive documents. Demonstrates lateral movement potential.

steps.observation: Testing complete. All sessions closed. No persistence installed.
steps.thought: Clean-up is mandatory. I must leave no traces, tools, or backdoors. Document every action taken for the report.
steps.action: Remove all uploaded payloads from target systems. Close all sessions. Restore any modified registry keys to original values. Verify clean-up with the client's SOC.
steps.expected_result: All artefacts removed. Client SOC confirms no unauthorised processes remain. Clean-up log signed by both parties.

scenario: Produce a professional penetration test report that communicates findings to both executive and technical audiences, with clear remediation guidance.

steps.observation: Testing complete. Three critical findings, four high, two medium.
steps.thought: The report has two audiences: the CISO (executive summary, business risk) and the IT team (technical detail, remediation steps). One report, two sections.
steps.action: Write executive summary (2 pages max): business impact of each critical finding in plain language, risk rating, estimated remediation cost. No technical jargon.
steps.expected_result: Executive summary approved by lead tester. Business impact clearly stated: 'SQL injection allows attacker to steal all 50,000 customer records — GDPR breach risk.'

steps.observation: Executive summary complete. Now write the technical section.
steps.thought: Each finding needs: vulnerability description, evidence (screenshot/log), CVSS score, remediation steps specific to their environment, and a retest recommendation.
steps.action: For each finding: (1) describe vulnerability class, (2) show proof-of-concept (screenshot, not actual data), (3) provide CVSS v3.1 score, (4) write specific remediation steps (e.g., 'Add parameterised queries to login.php line 47'), (5) recommend retest within 30 days.
steps.expected_result: Technical report with 9 findings, each with CVSS score, evidence, and specific remediation. SQL injection rated CVSS 9.8 Critical.

scenario: A SOC analyst receives an EDR alert: mass file encryption activity detected on WKSTN-042. The analyst must contain the incident, assess scope, and initiate recovery — reasoning through each decision before acting.
chapter: Multi-turn Agentic Incident Response: Ransomware Containment
source: MITRE ATT&CK Defender Playbook — Ransomware Response

steps.observation: EDR alert: spoolsv.exe spawning cmd.exe; 3,400 files renamed to .enc in under 2 minutes on WKSTN-042. Alert severity: CRITICAL.
steps.thought: This pattern — a system process spawning cmd.exe and rapid file renaming — is a strong ransomware indicator. Before anything else I need to contain the host to stop lateral spread. Isolating the machine is reversible; waiting is not.
steps.action: TOOL: isolate_host(host='WKSTN-042', reason='suspected_ransomware'). Disable NIC via EDR console. Document isolation timestamp.
steps.expected_result: WKSTN-042 network-isolated. No further outbound or lateral traffic possible from this host.

steps.observation: Host isolated. Need to understand scope: was this a single infection or has it spread?
steps.thought: I should check three things before declaring scope: (1) Did any SMB connections originate from WKSTN-042 in the last 30 minutes? (2) Are any other hosts showing similar file-rename patterns? (3) Did any shadow copies get deleted? I'll query the SIEM for lateral movement indicators.
steps.action: TOOL: siem_query(query='source_host=WKSTN-042 AND (event_type=SMB_connect OR event_type=shadow_copy_delete) timerange=30m'). Separately: TOOL: edr_hunt(ioc_type=mass_file_rename, threshold=100_files_per_min, timerange=1h, scope=all_hosts).
steps.expected_result: SIEM: WKSTN-042 connected to FILESERVER-01 via SMB 18 minutes ago. EDR: no other hosts show mass rename. Shadow copy delete command (vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet) detected on WKSTN-042 at T-22 minutes.

steps.observation: FILESERVER-01 was accessed from WKSTN-042 via SMB. Must check FILESERVER-01 for encryption activity.
steps.thought: The ransomware may have encrypted shares on FILESERVER-01 as well. I need to check FILESERVER-01 for similar file-rename activity and consider isolating it too — but FILESERVER-01 may be critical infrastructure, so I should check before isolating.
steps.action: TOOL: edr_query(host='FILESERVER-01', event_type='file_rename', timerange=30m). Check if encryption is active or completed. If active: TOOL: isolate_host(host='FILESERVER-01'). If completed: do NOT isolate — preserve for forensics and recovery assessment.
steps.expected_result: FILESERVER-01 shows 200 files renamed in the last 20 minutes — encryption appears to have stopped (ransomware note left). Isolate FILESERVER-01 to prevent any remaining processes.

steps.observation: Both WKSTN-042 and FILESERVER-01 isolated. Shadow copies deleted on WKSTN-042. Need to assess recovery options.
steps.thought: Shadow copies on WKSTN-042 are gone — so I cannot recover from VSS. I need to check: (1) Do we have an immutable backup of FILESERVER-01 that predates the attack? (2) When was the last successful backup? (3) Does the backup system itself appear uncompromised?
steps.action: TOOL: backup_query(target='FILESERVER-01', status=verified, max_age=48h). Check that the backup server's logs show no encryption activity. Verify backup integrity with test restore of a single directory.
steps.expected_result: Last clean backup of FILESERVER-01: 14 hours ago. Backup server unaffected (not on same network segment). Test restore of /finance/Q4-reports: success. Recovery is feasible.

steps.observation: Recovery path confirmed. Need to communicate status and begin clean rebuild.
steps.thought: Before restoring from backup I should: preserve forensic images of both affected hosts, notify stakeholders, and determine root cause. The initial entry point appears to be spoolsv.exe — this is consistent with PrintNightmare (CVE-2021-34527). I need to check if Print Spooler patches are applied before bringing hosts back.
steps.action: Create forensic image of WKSTN-042 and FILESERVER-01 disk. TOOL: vuln_check(host=all, cve='CVE-2021-34527'). Prepare clean OS rebuild for WKSTN-042. Restore FILESERVER-01 from verified backup. Apply emergency Print Spooler patch or disable service on all hosts before reconnection.
steps.expected_result: Forensic images captured. Vulnerability scan: 12 hosts have unpatched PrintNightmare. Emergency patching or service disablement applied. FILESERVER-01 restored in 4 hours. WKSTN-042 rebuilt from gold image.

scenario: A threat hunter reviewing Windows Security event logs notices an unusual spike in Event ID 4769 (Kerberos Service Ticket requests) using RC4 encryption. The hunter must determine if this is Kerberoasting, scope the activity, and harden the environment.
chapter: Multi-turn Agentic Threat Hunting: Kerberos Ticket Abuse
source: MITRE ATT&CK Defender Playbook — Kerberoasting Detection and Response

steps.observation: SIEM dashboard: 847 Event ID 4769 events in the last 2 hours, 90% with Ticket Encryption Type 0x17 (RC4-HMAC). Normal baseline: ~30 per hour. Source: a single workstation account 'jsmith'.
steps.thought: Event ID 4769 with RC4 encryption type (0x17) is the primary detection signal for Kerberoasting (MITRE T1558.003). An attacker who compromised jsmith's account is likely requesting service tickets for SPN-registered service accounts and exporting them for offline password cracking. I need to: (1) confirm the anomaly is real, (2) identify which SPNs were requested, (3) determine if jsmith is legitimately doing this.
steps.action: TOOL: siem_query(event_id=4769, encryption_type=0x17, account='jsmith', timerange=2h, fields='service_name,service_sid,client_address'). Cross-reference with jsmith's normal activity baseline.
steps.expected_result: 847 requests for 23 distinct SPNs including: MSSQLSvc/db01.corp:1433, HTTP/intranet.corp, IIS AppPool accounts. jsmith's normal activity: 2-3 ticket requests per day, only to Exchange. This is Kerberoasting.

steps.observation: Confirmed Kerberoasting from jsmith's account. 23 SPNs targeted. Need to assess whether any cracking has succeeded.
steps.thought: The attacker has the encrypted service tickets — cracking depends on password complexity. I should: (1) check if any of the targeted service accounts have shown unusual authentication, (2) check if jsmith's workstation shows offline cracking tools, (3) prioritise the most at-risk service accounts for immediate password rotation.
steps.action: TOOL: siem_query(account=service_accounts_list, event_type='authentication_success', timerange=4h, flag_unusual_source=true). TOOL: edr_query(host='WKSTN-jsmith', process_names=['hashcat','john','ntdsutil'], timerange=2h). Identify which targeted service accounts have weak passwords via AD password age and complexity policy check.
steps.expected_result: No unusual authentications yet — attack likely still in cracking phase. EDR: no hashcat on local machine, likely offloaded to attacker infra. MSSQLSvc account password: 12 characters, set 3 years ago, no complexity enforcement — HIGH RISK. IIS AppPool passwords: 20+ characters, rotated monthly — lower risk.

steps.observation: MSSQLSvc account is high-risk (old, simple password). jsmith's workstation may be compromised.
steps.thought: Immediate priorities in order: (1) Rotate the MSSQLSvc password NOW before cracking succeeds — this is time-critical. (2) Isolate jsmith's workstation to stop further ticket requests. (3) Force-reset jsmith's AD password. (4) After containment, investigate how jsmith's credentials were compromised.
steps.action: TOOL: ad_reset_password(account='MSSQLSvc', new_password=generate_random(length=30, complexity=high), update_service_configs=true). TOOL: isolate_host(host='WKSTN-jsmith'). TOOL: ad_reset_password(account='jsmith', force_reset_on_next_login=true, notify_user=true).
steps.expected_result: MSSQLSvc password rotated. All existing Kerberoasted tickets for MSSQLSvc are now worthless (new password = new hash). jsmith isolated. Risk window closed for highest-priority account.

steps.observation: Immediate risk contained. Now need to harden the environment to prevent recurrence.
steps.thought: Kerberoasting works because (a) RC4 encryption is allowed, (b) any domain user can request any SPN's ticket, and (c) service accounts have crackable passwords. Long-term fixes: convert all service accounts to gMSAs, enforce AES-256 Kerberos, and monitor for RC4 ticket requests permanently.
steps.action: TOOL: ad_audit(find_accounts=SPN_registered AND NOT gMSA AND NOT machine_account). For each: plan gMSA migration. Apply GPO: Network security: Configure encryption types allowed for Kerberos = AES128_HMAC_SHA1, AES256_HMAC_SHA1 ONLY (disable RC4). Add SIEM alert: Event 4769 with encryption_type=0x17 → HIGH alert, no exceptions.
steps.expected_result: 14 service accounts identified for gMSA migration. RC4 disabled via GPO on all domain computers. SIEM alert created. Kerberoasting surface reduced to near-zero.

scenario: Defending against CAPEC-1 - Accessing Functionality Not Properly Constrained by ACLs

steps.observation: Telemetry suggests an attacker may be in the "Explore" phase of CAPEC-1: [Survey] The attacker surveys the target application, possibly as a valid and authenticated user
steps.thought: Confirm intent and scope before triggering containment.
steps.action: Watch for the following activity patterns: [Survey] The attacker surveys the target application, possibly as a valid and authenticated user; [Identify Functionality] At each step, the attacker notes the resource or functionality access mechanism invoked upon performing specific actions; [Iterate over access capabilities] Possibly as a valid user, the attacker then tries to access each of the noted access mechanisms directly in order to perform functions not constrained by the ACLs.
steps.expected_result: Validated alert with attacker phase identified.

steps.observation: Detection confirmed. Need to interrupt the attack chain before objectives are met.
steps.thought: Limit blast radius while preserving forensic evidence.
steps.action: Isolate affected assets, revoke active sessions, and block indicators observed during detection.
steps.expected_result: Attacker loses access while evidence is preserved.

steps.observation: Containment in place. Need durable controls to prevent recurrence of CAPEC-1.
steps.thought: Apply defence-in-depth so a single bypass does not re-enable the pattern.
steps.action: Implement the following CAPEC mitigations: In a J2EE setting, administrators can associate a role that is impossible for the authenticator to grant users, such as "NoAccess", with all Servlets to which access is guarded by a limited number of servlets visible to, and accessible by, the user. Having done so, any direct access to those protected Servlets will be prohibited by the web container. In a more general setting, the administrator must mark every resource besides the ones supposed to be exposed to the user as accessible by a role impossible for the user to assume. The default security setting must be to deny access and then…
steps.expected_result: Attack pattern preconditions removed across the asset class.

In this attack the adversary looks for an application that loads the content of an environment variable into a buffer.; [Find injection vector] The adversary identifies an injection vector to deliver the excessive content to the targeted application's buffer.; [Craft overflow content] The adversary crafts the content to be injected. steps.action: Implement the following CAPEC mitigations: One solution to this problem is to protect encrypted data with a CRC of some sort. If knowing who last manipulated the data is important, then using a cryptographic "message authentication code" (or hMAC) is prescribed. However, this guidance is not a panacea. In particular, any value created by (and therefore encrypted by) the client, which itself is a "malicious" value, all the protective cryptography in the world can't make the value 'correct' again. Put simply, if the client has control over the whole process of generating and encoding the value, then simply protecting its integrity…

steps.thought: Apply defence-in-depth so a single bypass does not re-enable the pattern.
steps.action: Implement the following CAPEC mitigations: To mitigate this type of an attack, explicit intents should be used whenever sensitive data is being sent. An explicit intent is delivered to a specific application as declared within the intent, whereas the Android operating system determines who receives an implicit intent which could potentially be a malicious application. If an implicit intent must be used, then it should be assumed that the intent will be received by an unknown application and any response should be treated accordingly. Implicit intents should never be used for inter-application communication.
steps.expected_result: Attack pattern preconditions removed across the asset class.
